From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [CAS registry number](/w/index.php?title=CAS_registry_number&redirect=no "CAS registry number"))

Chemical identifier

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Cascommonchemistry2022.png/250px-Cascommonchemistry2022.png)](/wiki/File:Cascommonchemistry2022.png)

Screenshot of the CAS Common Chemistry database with information about [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine") ([58-08-2](https://commonchemistry.cas.org/detail?cas_rn=58-08-2)).

A **CAS Registry Number**[[1]](#cite_note-1) (also referred to as **CAS RN**[[2]](#cite_note-2) or informally **CAS Number**) is a [unique identification number](/wiki/Unique_identifier "Unique identifier"), assigned by the [Chemical Abstracts Service](/wiki/Chemical_Abstracts_Service "Chemical Abstracts Service") (CAS) in the US to every [chemical substance](/wiki/Chemical_substance "Chemical substance") described in the open scientific literature, in order to index the substance in the **CAS Registry**. This registry includes all substances described since 1957, plus some substances from as far back as the early 1800s.[[3]](#cite_note-CAScontent-3) It is a [chemical database](/wiki/Chemical_database "Chemical database") that includes [organic](/wiki/Organic_compounds "Organic compounds") and [inorganic](/wiki/Inorganic_compounds "Inorganic compounds") compounds, [minerals](/wiki/Mineral "Mineral"), [isotopes](/wiki/Isotope "Isotope"), [alloys](/wiki/Alloys "Alloys"), mixtures, and nonstructurable materials (UVCBs - substances of **u**nknown or **v**ariable composition, **c**omplex reaction products, or **b**iological origin).[[4]](#cite_note-crc-4) CAS RNs are generally [serial numbers](/wiki/Serial_number "Serial number") (with a [check digit](/wiki/Check_digit "Check digit")), so they do not contain any information about the structures themselves the way [SMILES](/wiki/Simplified_molecular-input_line-entry_system "Simplified molecular-input line-entry system") and [InChI](/wiki/International_Chemical_Identifier "International Chemical Identifier") strings do.

The CAS Registry is an authoritative collection of disclosed chemical substance information. It identifies more than 204 million unique organic and inorganic substances and 69 million protein and DNA sequences,[[3]](#cite_note-CAScontent-3) plus additional information about each substance. It is updated with around 15,000 additional new substances daily.[[5]](#cite_note-5) A collection of almost 500 thousand CAS registry numbers is made available under a [CC BY-NC](/wiki/CC_BY-NC "CC BY-NC") license at ACS Commons Chemistry.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History and use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAS_Registry_Number&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History and use")]

Historically, chemicals have been identified by a wide variety of synonyms and properties. One of the biggest challenges in the early development of substance indexing, a task undertaken by the Chemical Abstracts Service, was in identifying if a substance in literature was new or if it had been previously discovered. Well-known chemicals may be known via multiple generic, historical, commercial, and/or [(black)-market](/wiki/Black_market "Black market") names, and even [systematic nomenclature](/wiki/Chemical_nomenclature "Chemical nomenclature") based on structure alone was not universally useful. An algorithm was developed to translate the [structural formula](/wiki/Structural_formula "Structural formula") of a chemical into a computer-searchable table, which provided a basis for the service that listed each chemical with its CAS Registry Number, the CAS Chemical Registry System, which became operational in 1965.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

CAS Registry Numbers (CAS RN) are simple and regular, convenient for database searches. They offer a reliable, common and international link to every specific substance across the various nomenclatures and disciplines used by branches of science, industry, and regulatory bodies. Almost all molecule databases today allow searching by CAS Registry Number, and it is used as a global standard.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAS_Registry_Number&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Format")]

A CAS Registry Number has no inherent meaning, but is assigned in sequential, increasing order when the substance is identified by CAS scientists for inclusion in the CAS Registry database.

A CAS RN is separated by hyphens into three parts, the first consisting from two up to seven digits,[[9]](#cite_note-9) the second consisting of two digits, and the third consisting of a single digit serving as a [check digit](/wiki/Check_digit "Check digit"). This format gives CAS a maximum capacity of 1,000,000,000 unique numbers.

The check digit is found by taking the last digit times 1, the preceding digit times 2, the preceding digit times 3 etc., adding all these up and computing the sum [modulo](/wiki/Modular_arithmetic "Modular arithmetic") 10. For example, the CAS number of [water](/wiki/Water "Water") is 7732-18-5: the checksum 5 is calculated as (8×1 + 1×2 + 2×3 + 3×4 + 7×5 + 7×6) = 105; 105 mod 10 = 5.

## Granularity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAS_Registry_Number&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Granularity")]

* [Stereoisomers](/wiki/Stereoisomer "Stereoisomer") and [racemic mixtures](/wiki/Racemic_mixture "Racemic mixture") are assigned discrete CAS Registry Numbers: L-[epinephrine](/wiki/Epinephrine "Epinephrine") has 51-43-4, D-epinephrine has 150-05-0, and racemic DL-epinephrine has 329-65-7
* Different [phases](/wiki/Phase_(matter) "Phase (matter)") do not receive different CAS RNs (liquid water and ice both have 7732-18-5), but different crystal structures do ([carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon") in general is 7440-44-0, [graphite](/wiki/Graphite "Graphite") is 7782-42-5 and [diamond](/wiki/Diamond "Diamond") is 7782-40-3)
* Commonly encountered mixtures of known or unknown composition may receive a CAS RN; examples are [Leishman stain](/wiki/Leishman_stain "Leishman stain") (12627-53-1) and [mustard oil](/wiki/Mustard_oil "Mustard oil") (8007-40-7).
* Some [chemical elements](/wiki/Chemical_elements "Chemical elements") are discerned by their oxidation state, e.g. the element [chromium](/wiki/Chromium "Chromium") has 7440-47-3, the trivalent Cr(III) has 16065-83-1 and the [hexavalent Cr(VI) species](/wiki/Hexavalent_chromium "Hexavalent chromium") have 18540-29-9.
* Occasionally whole classes of molecules receive a single CAS RN: the class of enzymes known as [alcohol dehydrogenases](/wiki/Alcohol_dehydrogenase "Alcohol dehydrogenase") has 9031-72-5.

## Search engines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAS_Registry_Number&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Search engines")]

* CHEMINDEX Search via [Canadian Centre for Occupational Health and Safety](/wiki/Canadian_Centre_for_Occupational_Health_and_Safety "Canadian Centre for Occupational Health and Safety")[[10]](#cite_note-cis-10)
* ChemIDplus Advanced via [United States National Library of Medicine](/wiki/United_States_National_Library_of_Medicine "United States National Library of Medicine")[[11]](#cite_note-adv-11)
* Common Chemistry[[12]](#cite_note-ss-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) via Australian Inventory of Chemical Substances[[14]](#cite_note-adhg-14)
* [European chemical Substances Information System](/wiki/European_chemical_Substances_Information_System "European chemical Substances Information System")[[15]](#cite_note-eecs-15) via the website of [Royal Society of Chemistry](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_Chemistry "Royal Society of Chemistry")[[16]](#cite_note-fcrn-16)
* HSNO Chemical Classification Information Database via [Environmental Risk Management Authority](/wiki/Environmental_Risk_Management_Authority "Environmental Risk Management Authority")[[17]](#cite_note-hcci-17)
* Search Tool of Australian Inventory of Chemical Substances[[18]](#cite_note-ast-18)
* USEPA CompTox Chemicals Dashboard[[19]](#cite_note-19)
